title: Cat Dry Crust in Eye Corners: Causes, Urgency, and Care Guide
meta_desc: Learn about dry crust in your cat's eyes, whether it is an emergency, and why sharing a photo helps your vet provide the best triage advice.
content_html:
What Is the Dry Crust in Your Cat's Eyes?

Dry, dark-colored crust in the corners of a cat's eyes is very common and is often just dried tear film, dust, and natural debris. In healthy cats, tears are produced constantly to lubricate the eye; when these tears evaporate or drain toward the corner of the eye, they leave behind a residue that hardens into a reddish-brown or black crust. This is usually more noticeable in cats with light-colored fur or flatter faces.

Is It an Emergency?

Urgency Level: Low. Generally, a small amount of dry crusting is not an emergency and can be managed with routine cleaning. However, if you notice the crusting is accompanied by yellow or green discharge, squinting, rubbing at the face, or significant redness of the white part of the eye, you should schedule a non-emergency appointment with your veterinarian to rule out infections or corneal issues.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a high-quality, close-up photo of your cat's eyes is one of the best tools for veterinary triage. A photo allows a professional to see the exact color and texture of the discharge and assess whether the eye itself looks clear or irritated. By seeing the eye through a photo, a vet can quickly determine if the issue is a normal part of aging and grooming or if it requires a clinical examination.

faqs.question: How should I clean the crust away?
faqs.answer: Use a soft cloth or cotton ball dampened with lukewarm water. Gently wipe from the inner corner of the eye outward, being careful not to touch the eyeball itself.

faqs.question: Could my cat have allergies?
faqs.answer: Yes, environmental allergies to dust, pollen, or mold can cause increased tear production, leading to more frequent crusting in the corners of the eyes.

faqs.question: When should I be worried about eye discharge?
faqs.answer: Seek veterinary advice if the discharge becomes thick and pus-like, if the eye looks cloudy, or if your cat seems to be in pain or light-sensitive.
